---
title: 'Exam Template: Statistical Inference'
author: ""
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  word_document: default
  html_document: default
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
# do not change these options
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE) # do not edit this line.
knitr::opts_chunk$set(error = TRUE,comment=NA) # do not edit this line.
```

# INSTRUCTIONS

Save this template as your studentID.Rmd; you will upload this file as part of your submission. Change the author information on line 3 of this file to your **student ID**. Do not change the authorship to your name.

Your should knit this file to a document **Word** format. The Word document is what will be marked!

**To conform to reproducible research principles you should submit both your .Rmd document and your Word document.**

Any changes that you make to the data (e.g. variable name changes) should be made entirely within R.

The subsections labelled **Answer:** indicate where you should put in your written Answers. The template also provides blank code chunks for you to complete your Answers; you may choose to add additional chunks if required.

This is an individual assessment: do not work with any other person during this exam.

You must submit your assignment before the stated deadline by electronic submission through Blackboard.

- It is a good idea to save your work early and frequently to ensure you have no issues with the submission portal. Multiple submissions can be made to the portal, only the final one will be marked.

- It is your responsibility to submit the exam in a format stipulated above. Your marks may be affected if your tutor cannot open or properly view your submission.

- Do not leave submission to the very last minute. Always allow time in case of technical issues.

- It is essential that you check that you have submitted the correct file(s), and that each complete file was received. Submission receipts are accessed from the Coursework tab.

There is no late submission permitted on this assessment. Ensure that you submit your submission in good time. Neither the module leader nor module team can accept late assessments, do not ask them to do so.

## Use of generative artificial intelligence 

Appropriate use of generative artificial intelligence (AI) is permitted as a tool to assist in learning and assessment, but not to complete the assessment for you. AI responses are typically generic and poorly structured - including material that is not fully relevant to the question and approaches that are not fully justified, thus responses heavily reliant on AI often score poorly. You must demonstrate your own understanding, and that that you can add value as a data professional – this can be through the decision making process, reproducible and transparent research, cohesion of arguments, appropriate context and originality. Use the material we provide on the course, and the statistical methods we suggest on the course. Inclusion of material out of scope and inconsistencies with teaching of the course is often assessed to be poor understanding from over-reliance of AI. Ensure that you are correctly attributing any use of AI in the assessment, failure to do so can lead to referral for assessment offences.

**You should use concepts and techniques introduced within the module delivery.** 


# SCENARIO FOR EXAM

## Background to the research

The client is university management who are looking to understand support required for students with English not as their first language. 

Data has been obtained on a voluntary basis from students on a postgraduate course. They were asked a series of questions about themselves, and asked to take an assessment of their English reading, writing, speaking and listening abilities.

## Data instructions

Your individual data set is accessed via Blackboard >>> Assessments >>> Dewis Data For Exam.

You must only analyse the specified data. No other data is to be used for this assessment.

All data manipulation and analyses must be done within R.

## Data structure

consent - If student gives 'Consent' for their data to be analysed for research purposes or 'Refused'

globalM - If student identifies as Global Majority 'Yes' or not 'No'. See: https://www.uwe.ac.uk/about/values-vision-strategy/equality-diversity-and-inclusivity/anti-racism

confidence - Self-assessed level of proficiency with the English language, selected using a sliding scale (higher values mean higher confidence)

friends - Estimated number of friends or family members proficient with the English language

year - Year of birth

gender - Gender

read - Reading ability score

write - Writing ability score

speak - Speaking ability score

listen - Listening ability score


# QUESTIONS START HERE

## Data Preparation 

1. Ensure that you have prepared your submission as per Instructions.

2. Prepare the data for analyses.

You should explain any decisions with respect to:

-any manipulation of the data structure

-handling of groups with low frequency counts

-unusual or missing values.

Marks will be awarded for logical actions performed, and conforming to principles of reproducible research in data preparation and throughout. 

**(weighting 30%)**


### Answer:
```{r data}

```

## Data Preparation Decisions:

### Data Manipulation:

I created a new variable called total_stat, which gave me the summation of people who are confident, people who have friends and people who can read, write, speak and Listen in English Language.

### Handling Low Frequency Groups:

GlobalM variable contains 4 values(Yes, No, Missing, Undisc), which i then regrouped the Missing/Undisc values as NA, so as to make our data more clean. I now have 3 values in my globalM variable(Yes, No and NA).

The Year variable contains 2 digits values instead of the 4 digits values which the year should have, i then regrouped them by changing the 2 digits values to NA. My data now contains just the year(1990 - 1995) and then NA.

### Data Structure Manipulation

I converted some categorical variables to their proper type, so they can have proper statistical treatment.



## Core Research Question: Model building

Create a linear regression model showing factors that can impact student confidence in their English language ability.

Your answer should include:

-	justification for the attempted statistical model, including any additional supporting exploratory data analyses used to make the choice;

-	interpretation of the model and the validity of model assumptions.

Your answer should be in the form of a concise report, suitable for the client. Maximum number of words: 500

**(weighting 35%)**

### Answer: 



```{r core}

```

## Linear regression Report

### Factors That Can Impact Student Confidence In Their English Language Ability.

Justification: Linear regression model was used to identify factors that influences student's confidence. In my exploratory analysis, it is shown that there is a strong connection between students confidence and their ability to read and write(write: r = 0.644, read: r = 0.591), and minimal correlation with their listening level.

Assumption Validation:
Linearity: This plots shows they are spread across evenly.

Normality of Residuals: The points follow the diagonal line, which suggests that residual are normally distributed.

Homogenity of Variance: Residual plots also showed evenly scattered plots.

Posterior Predictive Check: This suggest that the model is a good fit.

Influential Observations: Since most lines are within contour lines and scattered between the lines, this indicates that there are no extreme influencial data points distorting the model.


## Your Research Question: Bivariate analysis


Clearly state a **new** research question based on the data set supplied, not answered in your analyses above. You should consider a research question that will **make use of exactly two of the variables** from the available data. 

State and action a statistical analyses plan.

It may be appropriate to include the following: exploratory data analyses; hypothesis testing; interpretation; discussion of limitations.

Your answer should be in the form of a concise report, suitable for the client. Maximum number of words: 500


**(weighting 35%)**

### Answer:


```{r your}

```

## Bivariate Analysis Report: Global Majority and being able to Speak.

Research Question: Is there any relationship between the ability to speak English Language and being part of global majority?
  
Rationale: The ability to Speak English does not determine the level of one's confidence and ultimately being part of the global majority.

Analytical Approach:
1. Exploratory Analysis: I visualized mean of speaking level by global majority with 95% confidence intervals.
2. Statistical Test: One-way ANOVA Test was carried out to compare speaking by global majority.

Limitations:
The error bars indicates the confidence interval around each mean. They overlap significantly for all three groups, suggesting that there is no statistically significant difference in the average speaking levels between those who are part of the global majority, those who are not, or those in the NA category.

Key Findings:
This analysis suggests that identifying as part of the global majority does not have any substantial impact on the average speaking level.


# End matter - Session Information

Do not edit this part. Make sure that you compile your document so that the information about your session (including software / package versions) is included in your submission.

```{r}
sessionInfo()
```
